title: GitLab の 429 エラー：原因と解決策
reasons: The article accurately describes the meaning of GitLab 429 errors, common causes, and methods for checking rate limits via API headers and admin settings.

However, the default API rate limits presented (600 requests/min for authenticated, 300 requests/min for unauthenticated) are oversimplified and not universally applicable across all GitLab APIs, which could mislead users.
one or more claims are unsupported or unverifiable
required_actions: Clarify that the mentioned default rate limits (600/300) are examples for specific API endpoints (e.g., `/project/:id/jobs`) and that actual rate limits can vary significantly by API, authentication status, and GitLab instance configuration.
Advise readers to consult the official GitLab documentation for specific endpoint rate limits or to rely on response headers for current usage.
Add official or primary source URLs for unsupported claims.

sources.title: Rate limitations announced for Projects, Groups, and Users APIs - GitLab
sources.claim: GitLab's 429 error means 'Too Many Requests' and indicates that the GitLab API rate limit has been reached, occurring when requests exceed allowed limits.

sources.title: Rate Limiting - The GitLab Handbook
sources.claim: GitLab's 429 error means 'Too Many Requests' and indicates that the GitLab API rate limit has been reached, occurring when requests exceed allowed limits. Rate limiting is a critical feature that enhances security and performance by restricting the number of requests users or IP addresses can make within a set timeframe, including those from CI/CD pipelines.

sources.title: Authenticated user rate limits - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: GitLab's 429 error means 'Too Many Requests' and indicates that the GitLab API rate limit has been reached, occurring when requests exceed allowed limits from users or CI/CD pipelines (REST and GraphQL API calls, including requests from integrations, CI/CD jobs, and automation scripts).

sources.title: Rate limits - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: For the `project/:id/jobs` endpoint, a rate limit of 600 calls per authenticated user per minute is applied by default.

sources.title: レート制限 - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: For the `project/:id/jobs` endpoint, a rate limit of 600 calls per authenticated user per minute is applied by default.

sources.title: ユーザーとIPレート制限 - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: For the `project/:id/jobs` endpoint, a rate limit of 600 calls per authenticated user per minute is applied by default.

sources.title: Rate limitations announced for Projects, Groups, and Users APIs - GitLab
sources.claim: GitLab administrators can check and adjust instance-wide rate limits via the `/api/v4/admin/application_settings` endpoint.

sources.title: Application settings API - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: GitLab administrators can check and adjust instance-wide rate limits via the `/api/v4/admin/application_settings` endpoint, and must have administrator access to the instance to use this API.

sources.title: GitLab CI/CD job token - ETSI Labs
sources.claim: The `$CI_JOB_TOKEN` used in CI/CD pipelines is a job-specific token. For example, in the context of the package registry, specific rate limits apply (e.g., 2000 requests per minute per user for authenticated requests).

sources.title: A solution to gitlab package registry 429's errors - Olivier's Blog
sources.claim: The `$CI_JOB_TOKEN` used in CI/CD pipelines is a job-specific token, and for the package registry, specific rate limits apply (e.g., 500 requests per minute for unauthenticated IPs, 2000 requests per minute per user for authenticated).

sources.title: Rate limiting: 429 Too Many Requests (#1966) · Issue · gitlab-org/gitlab-runner
sources.claim: The `$CI_JOB_TOKEN` used in CI/CD pipelines is a job-specific token, and does not apply to JWT requests authenticated by `gitlab-ci-token` for Git and container registry failed authentication bans.

sources.title: GraphQL API - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: When using the GraphQL API, a different rate limiting model based on query complexity is adopted compared to the REST API, where the GraphQL API scores the complexity of a query to protect against performance impacts.

sources.title: Rate Limiting - The GitLab Handbook
sources.claim: When using the GraphQL API, a different rate limiting model based on query complexity is adopted compared to the REST API; GraphQL endpoints currently do not return rate limit response headers.

sources.title: Application settings API - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: If you have administrator privileges, you can check instance-level limits with the command `curl -H "PRIVATE-TOKEN: <admin-token>" "https://gitlab.example.com/api/v4/admin/application_settings"` (the `grep -i rate` part is for filtering).

sources.title: Authenticated user rate limits - GitLab Docs
sources.claim: User-level limit status can be checked from the response header of any API call; GitLab includes rate limit information in response headers for all requests (`RateLimit-Limit`, `RateLimit-Remaining`, etc.).

sources.title: docs/rate-limiting/README.md · f60467a699203dec219b11fd4190be0e969f748a - GitLab
sources.claim: User-level limit status can be checked from the response header of any API call; headers like `RateLimit-Observed` and `RateLimit-Remaining` are returned.

sources.title: Does gitlab API have a rate limit - Stack Overflow
sources.claim: User-level limit status can be checked from the response header of any API call; API responses include headers like `RateLimit-Limit`, `RateLimit-Observed`, and `RateLimit-Remaining`.

unsupported_claims: デフォルトでは、認証ユーザーは（すべてのAPIで）1分間に600リクエストという制限が適用されます。
デフォルトでは、未認証の場合は（すべてのAPIで）1分間に300リクエストという制限が適用されます。
